it.title: Closed Form for the Imaginary Part of $\text{Li}_3\Big(\frac{1+i}2\Big)$

truth.notes: Main quantity: Im[Li_3((1+i)/2)], the imaginary part of the trilogarithm at (1+i)/2. Cleo expresses it via the hypergeometric 4F3(1/2,1/2,1,1; 3/2,3/2,3/2; 1) (equivalently sum_{n>=0} 1/((2n+1)^3 binom(2n,n)^{-1}...), evaluated with mp.hyper). Verified two ways: (a) mpmath's mp.polylog(3,(1+i)/2) and (b) the defining power series sum z^n/n^3 computed at 60 dps with 400 terms; both agree with the closed form to all 40 significant digits. Cleo's answer has a second part: Im[Li_3(1+i)] = 3*pi^3/64 + (pi/16)ln^2 2 - (1/4)*4F3(...;1), which I also verified numerically (= 1.267083441888923963686650200213485875911, matches mp.polylog(3,1+1j) to 40 digits) and which is consistent with the identity in the question, Im Li_3((1+i)/2) + Im Li_3(1+i) = 7pi^3/128 + (3/32)pi ln^2 2. No doubt about the ground truth; the only caveat is that the closed form is 'closed' only if 4F3 at unit argument is accepted as closed form, as Cleo herself notes.

solve.summary: I proved rigorously (dilogarithm inversion with full branch control, then trilogarithm inversion by differentiation, specialized at z=1+i where 1/z=(1-i)/2) the exact reduction Im Li3((1+i)/2) = 7pi^3/128 + 3pi ln^2(2)/32 - Im Li3(1+i), plus the equivalent representations (1/2)Int_0^1 ln^2(1-x)/(1+x^2) dx and Sum sin(n pi/4)/(2^(n/2) n^3); both sides were computed by independent methods (own series summation, tanh-sinh quadrature, continuation-free integral representation) and agree to 139 significant digits. I further proved that the entire anharmonic-orbit functional-equation system has rank 2 on the imaginary parts and can only ever determine the sum Im Li3((1+i)/2) + Im Li3(1+i), never each constant separately. Exhaustive PSLQ at 130-digit precision excludes any rational relation of the target with pi^3, pi ln^2 2, G ln2, zeta(3), beta'(2), Gamma(1/4)/gamma constants, Ti3(sqrt2±1), ln(1+sqrt2)-products, Cl2(pi/4)-products and level-8 L-derivatives, consistent with this constant's accepted status as an irreducible weight-3 level-4 basis generator in the multiple-polylogarithm literature. Status is set to "partial" out of honesty: the reduction is completely derived and verified far beyond 15 digits, but a genuinely classical closed form is believed not to exist, so the final expression renames the constant (via Im Li3(1+i)) rather than eliminating it. Any correct equivalent ground-truth form must equal 0.5700774070887689781956... which is what I verified.

Every analytic step (DCT interchanges, arc estimates, edge limits, zero counting, boundary asymptotics) is justified in the solution file, and all intermediate identities were independently checked numerically (residue formula to 50 digits, winding number = 1, antiderivative FD check, reassembly to 60 digits).
